---
title: "26 · Q-Learning: TD for Optimal Action Values, Off-Policy"
subtitle: "Replace the on-policy next action with the max — estimates q* regardless of the data-collection policy."
lesson-id: "mfrl.q-learning"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.n-step-sarsa"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Sarsa update from memory? — see [MFRL — 24, Sarsa](../24-sarsa/index.qmd).
2. Bellman *optimality* equation $q^\*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma \max_{a'} q^\*(s', a')]$? — see [MFRL — 10, Bellman Optimality](../10-bellman-optimality-equation/index.qmd).
3. Difference between $q_\pi$ and $q^\*$ (one is for a fixed policy, the
   other is the best you can do)?
4. ε-greedy policy? — see [MFRL — 19, ε-Greedy](../19-epsilon-greedy/index.qmd).

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

A 5×5 grid with a goal at the top-right and a "lava" cell that gives
reward $-10$. Discount $\gamma = 0.9$.

The agent **acts uniformly at random** (every step picks a random direction,
no greedy bias). Yet **Q-learning's $q$-table converges to $q^\*$** — the
optimal action values — because Q-learning's target uses
$\max_{a'} q(s', a')$ regardless of which action the agent actually took.

Drag the slider on episodes; watch the heatmap of $\max_a q(s, a)$ light up
near the goal and dim near the lava.

```{ojs}
viewof qEpisodes = Inputs.range([10, 1500], {value: 400, step: 10, label: "Episodes:"})
viewof qSeed = Inputs.range([1, 30], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "Seed:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  function mulberry32(a) {
    return function() {
      a |= 0; a = a + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
      let t = a;
      t = Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 15, t | 1);
      t ^= t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, t | 61);
      return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
    };
  }
  const rand = mulberry32(qSeed * 23);
  const N = 5;
  const A = 4;     // 0=up, 1=down, 2=left, 3=right
  const gamma = 0.9, alpha = 0.2;
  const lava = {r: 2, c: 2};
  const goal = {r: 0, c: 4};
  const q = new Array(N * N * A).fill(0);
  const idx = (r, c, a) => (r * N + c) * A + a;
  function step(r, c, a) {
    let nr = r, nc = c;
    if (a === 0) nr = Math.max(0, r - 1);
    if (a === 1) nr = Math.min(N - 1, r + 1);
    if (a === 2) nc = Math.max(0, c - 1);
    if (a === 3) nc = Math.min(N - 1, c + 1);
    let reward = -0.04, done = false;
    if (nr === lava.r && nc === lava.c) { reward = -10; done = true; }
    if (nr === goal.r && nc === goal.c) { reward = 1; done = true; }
    return {nr, nc, reward, done};
  }
  for (let ep = 0; ep < qEpisodes; ep++) {
    let r = 0, c = 0;
    if (r === goal.r && c === goal.c) continue;
    for (let t = 0; t < 100; t++) {
      const a = Math.floor(rand() * A);   // FULLY RANDOM behaviour policy
      const {nr, nc, reward, done} = step(r, c, a);
      let maxQ = 0;
      if (!done) {
        maxQ = -Infinity;
        for (let ap = 0; ap < A; ap++) maxQ = Math.max(maxQ, q[idx(nr, nc, ap)]);
      }
      const target = reward + gamma * maxQ;
      q[idx(r, c, a)] += alpha * (target - q[idx(r, c, a)]);
      r = nr; c = nc;
      if (done) break;
    }
  }
  const cells = [];
  for (let r = 0; r < N; r++) for (let c = 0; c < N; c++) {
    let v = -Infinity;
    for (let a = 0; a < A; a++) v = Math.max(v, q[idx(r, c, a)]);
    cells.push({r, c, v});
  }
  const Plot = await import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm");
  return Plot.plot({
    width: 360, height: 360,
    color: {scheme: "viridis", legend: true},
    x: {label: "col"}, y: {label: "row", reverse: true},
    marks: [
      Plot.cell(cells, {x: "c", y: "r", fill: "v"}),
      Plot.text(cells, {x: "c", y: "r", text: d => d.v.toFixed(1), fill: "white"})
    ]
  });
}
```

The agent acts randomly. **Yet $q$ tells the story of optimal play.** That
asymmetry — *learn one policy while following another* — is the core of
**off-policy** learning.

## Wonder

1. Sarsa learns $q_\pi$ for whatever $\pi$ generated the data. Q-learning
   learns $q^\*$ regardless of the data policy. Why does swapping
   $q(s', a')$ for $\max_{a'} q(s', a')$ buy that?
2. The agent in the widget acts uniformly random — yet the heatmap shows
   the *optimal* policy. So can you learn from data collected by
   *anybody* — a human demonstrator, a bad policy, a random policy?
3. Q-learning's target is the Bellman *optimality* equation. Why does that
   matter? What does Sarsa's target look like in comparison?
4. If Q-learning learns $q^\*$ from any data, why ever bother with on-policy
   methods?

## Discover

Six problems to derive Q-learning, see the off-policy magic, and feel the
catch.

### Problem 1 — Bellman optimality recap

Write the Bellman optimality equation for $q^\*(s, a)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$q^\*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} q^\*(s', a') \,\bigg|\, s, a\right].$$
The expectation is over $r, s'$ from the dynamics — there is no policy in
sight on the right-hand side, because the optimal policy is "do the max."
</details>

### Problem 2 — the Q-learning target

Apply the Robbins-Monro recipe: a noisy sample of the right-hand side at
$(s, a)$ is...?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A noisy sample is $r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a')$, where $(r, s')$ is one
observed transition. The bootstrap uses the *current* $q$ in place of the
unknown $q^\*$. Update:
$$q(s, a) \leftarrow q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a') - q(s, a)].$$
This is **Q-learning**.
</details>

### Problem 3 — what's $a'$?

In Sarsa the target uses $q(s', a')$ where $a'$ is the *action actually
taken*. In Q-learning the target uses $\max_{a'} q(s', a')$. Why does this
make Q-learning *off-policy*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Did the agent ever take the action that achieves the max?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The action $a'$ in Q-learning's target is the *greedy* action under the
current $q$ — but the agent does not need to *take* it. The agent could
have acted ε-greedy, fully random, by demonstration — anything. The target
is computed from $q$ alone, not from the action taken. So the
**data-collection policy** (called the *behaviour policy*) and the
**evaluated policy** (the implicit greedy policy under $q$) are decoupled.
That is the definition of **[off-policy](#off-policy-glossary)**.
</details>

### Problem 4 — why this gives $q^\*$ and not $q_\pi$

Sketch why Q-learning targets $q^\*$ rather than $q_\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What is the fixed point of the operator
$\mathbb{E}[r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a') \mid s, a]$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The expected target is exactly the Bellman *optimality* operator $\mathcal{T}^\*$:
$(\mathcal{T}^\* q)(s, a) = \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a')]$.
Its unique fixed point is $q^\*$. So if Q-learning's iterate converges, it
converges to $q^\*$ — independently of the policy generating data.
**The "max" inside the target is the BOE**; that's why Q-learning learns
$q^\*$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — convergence

State a sufficient condition for tabular Q-learning to converge to $q^\*$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(Watkins-Dayan 1992):
(i) every $(s, a)$ visited infinitely often;
(ii) per-$(s,a)$ step sizes satisfy $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$,
$\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
Then $q \to q^\*$ a.s. **No GLIE requirement** — because Q-learning's target
doesn't care what the data-policy is, exploration just needs to cover.
</details>

### Problem 6 — the maximisation bias

A subtle catch. With finite samples, $\max_{a'} q(s', a')$ tends to
*overestimate* the true max. Why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Jensen's inequality: $\mathbb{E}[\max] \ge \max \mathbb{E}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathbb{E}[\max_a q(s', a)] \ge \max_a \mathbb{E}[q(s', a)] = \max_a q^\*(s', a)$. Because we max over noisy estimates, we systematically overshoot — the
**maximisation bias**. Double Q-learning (Hasselt 2010) fixes this by
maintaining two $q$-tables, one to *select* the argmax action and the other
to *evaluate* it. We won't expand here; see Hasselt's paper.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
The Sarsa-vs-Q-learning difference is *one symbol*: Sarsa uses
$q(s', a')$ (the action the policy chose); Q-learning uses
$\max_{a'} q(s', a')$ (the action the optimal policy would choose). That
single substitution swaps on-policy for off-policy and $q_\pi$ for $q^\*$.
:::

::: {.fun-fact}
Christopher **Watkins** introduced Q-learning in his 1989 Cambridge PhD
thesis. He proved convergence in the same thesis — the first proof of
its kind. The 2013 DeepMind paper that ignited deep RL used Q-learning
with a deep neural network and called the result *Deep Q-Network (DQN)*.
The 2015 *Nature* paper showing DQN reaching human-level Atari from raw
pixels is, at its core, Watkins's 1989 update with two engineering tricks
— target networks and replay buffers (covered in Lesson 31).
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Q-learning — off-policy TD(0) for optimal action values.** Given a
sample tuple $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ collected under *any* behaviour
policy $b$, update
$$
q(s_t, a_t) \;\leftarrow\; q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha_t \, [\,\underbrace{r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s_{t+1}, a')}_{\text{Q-learning target}} - q(s_t, a_t)\,].
$$
Under (i) every $(s, a)$ visited infinitely often and (ii) RM step
conditions, $q \to q^\*$ a.s. No constraint on $b$ except *coverage*.

**Off-policy** []{#off-policy-glossary} = the data-collection (behaviour)
policy may differ from the policy being evaluated/improved (target).

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Reward I just saw, plus the best of the next —*
> *Not the action I took, but the action I would.*
> *Behaviour and target part company,*
> *And I learn the best policy from any data at all.*

## Apply

### Worked example — Q-learning step on a tiny grid

> States $s_1, s_2$. From $s_1$ actions {right} and {left}. Right goes to
> $s_2$ (terminal, reward $+1$); left self-loops (reward $-1$). $\gamma = 0.9$,
> $\alpha = 0.5$. Initial $q = 0$. Suppose the behaviour policy chose left,
> giving the tuple $(s_1, \text{left}, r=-1, s_1)$. Compute the Q-learning
> update.

A competent solver thinks like this:

1. **Target:** $r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s_1, a') = -1 + 0.9 \cdot \max(q(s_1, \text{left}), q(s_1, \text{right})) = -1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = -1$.
2. **Update $q(s_1, \text{left})$:** $0 + 0.5 \cdot (-1 - 0) = -0.5$.
3. **Suppose next the behaviour policy *also* chooses left**: tuple
   $(s_1, \text{left}, r=-1, s_1)$ again.
4. Target: $-1 + 0.9 \cdot \max(-0.5, 0) = -1 + 0 = -1$. Update
   $q(s_1, \text{left}) = -0.5 + 0.5 \cdot (-0.5) = -0.75$.
5. Eventually random behaviour selects right and $q(s_1, \text{right})$
   gets updated. Even from purely **left-biased data** Q-learning would
   *eventually* learn that right is better — *as long as* right is sampled
   sometimes (the coverage condition).

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** What is the Q-learning target if $r = 2$, $\gamma = 0.95$,
$q(s', \text{up}) = 4, q(s', \text{down}) = 7, q(s', \text{stay}) = 5$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\max_{a'} q(s', a') = 7$. Target $= 2 + 0.95 \cdot 7 = 8.65$.
</details>

**W2.** True or false: "Q-learning needs ε-greedy behaviour to converge."

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**False — only coverage is needed.** Q-learning converges to $q^\*$ even
under uniformly random or expert-demonstration behaviour, *as long as
every $(s, a)$ is visited infinitely often.* The target doesn't care.
</details>

**W3.** Spot the difference: in Sarsa we required GLIE for $q \to q^\*$;
in Q-learning we don't. Why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sarsa learns $q_\pi$ for the *behaviour* policy $\pi$. So you need $\pi \to \pi^\*$ (the GLIE part) to get $q_\pi \to q^\*$. Q-learning learns
$q^\*$ directly via the BOE; the behaviour just supplies coverage.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show that Q-learning is Dvoretzky-shaped per $(s, a)$. What plays
the role of "contracting target"?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The expected target is the Bellman optimality operator.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$q(s,a)_{k+1} = (1 - \alpha) q(s,a)_k + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a')_k]$. The expectation of the bracket is $(\mathcal{T}^\* q_k)(s, a)$, where
$\mathcal{T}^\*$ is the BOE-operator and a $\gamma$-contraction in
$\ell_\infty$ (lesson 11). So target is contracting toward $q^\*$;
Dvoretzky's theorem applies; $q \to q^\*$ a.s.
</details>

**P2.** Sarsa under uniform-random behaviour: what does it converge to,
and why is that *not* $q^\*$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What policy generated the $a'$ in Sarsa's target?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sarsa's target uses the actually-taken $a'$ — uniform-random in this case.
So Sarsa learns $q_\pi$ where $\pi$ is uniform random — the *value of acting
randomly*. That's nowhere near $q^\*$ unless the MDP is degenerate.
**Lesson:** Sarsa's target inherits the behaviour policy; Q-learning's does
not.
</details>

**P3.** A continuous-action problem (e.g., robot torque). Why does
Q-learning's $\max_{a'}$ become a *practical* obstacle?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For continuous $a$, computing $\max_{a'} q(s', a')$ requires solving a
continuous optimisation at every step. That is hopeless for arbitrary $q$
and motivates *actor-critic* methods (Lessons 35-38), where a separate
parameterised actor proposes the argmax action and is itself trained by
gradient ascent on $q$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *The deadly triad preview.* Name the three ingredients that, when
combined, can cause Q-learning with function approximation to diverge.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Bootstrapping** (TD-style targets), **off-policy** learning, and
**function approximation** (parameterised $q$, especially neural nets).
Tabular Q-learning has the first two but not the third — and is safe.
Adding FA breaks the safety; this is **Baird's counterexample**, expanded
in Lesson 30. Modern deep-RL papers use *target networks* and *replay
buffers* to stabilise.
</details>

**S2.** *Maximisation bias quantified.* Suppose we have $K$ noisy estimators
$\hat q_1, \ldots, \hat q_K$ of $q$, each unbiased with variance $\sigma^2$.
Show $\mathbb{E}[\max_k \hat q_k]$ exceeds $\max_k q_k$ by at least
$\sigma \sqrt{\log K}$ (very roughly).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Order statistics of $K$ Gaussians.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, $\mathbb{E}[\max] \approx \mu + \sigma \sqrt{2 \log K}$ (extreme-value theory). Even if each estimator is
unbiased *individually*, their max is biased upward by $\Theta(\sigma \sqrt{\log K})$. **Q-learning systematically over-estimates** the value of the best
action, especially in high-action settings. Double Q-learning fixes this.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Q-learning is on-policy because the agent uses ε-greedy."** No —
   *acting* ε-greedy doesn't make the algorithm on-policy. What matters is
   what's in the *target*. Q-learning's target uses $\max$, not $a'$, so
   it's off-policy regardless of how you act.
2. **"Sarsa and Q-learning differ only by the data tuple."** Almost — Sarsa
   needs $a'$, Q-learning doesn't. But the more important difference is the
   *fixed point* they target ($q_\pi$ vs $q^\*$).
3. **"Off-policy means we don't need exploration."** No. We need *coverage*
   — every $(s, a)$ must be visited. Without coverage, you can't update some
   $q$ entries and the algorithm can't reach $q^\*$.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else:

1. **Q-learning update:** $q(s, a) \leftarrow q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a') - q(s, a)]$. The "max" is the BOE.
2. **Off-policy.** Behaviour policy and target policy can differ; only
   coverage is required.
3. **Targets $q^\*$ directly.** No GLIE needed — but watch out for
   maximisation bias and the deadly triad with FA.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you derive the Q-learning update from the BOE, on a blank page?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 8 practice problems without peeking past
   *Hint 1*?
3. Can you state, in one breath, why Q-learning is off-policy?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Lesson 27 — TD Methods Unified](../27-td-unified-view/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 — maximisation bias is the empirical face of
  Q-learning's failures.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problems 3 and 4. The "max in the target"
  is the load-bearing substitution.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[MFRL — 25, n-Step Sarsa](../25-n-step-sarsa/index.qmd)** — Q-learning
  is the off-policy cousin; same algebraic shape.
- **[MFRL — 10, Bellman Optimality Equation](../10-bellman-optimality-equation/index.qmd)** — the equation Q-learning solves.
- **[MFRL — 11, Contraction Mapping Theorem](../11-contraction-mapping-theorem/index.qmd)** — the BOE-operator's contraction property is what makes
  convergence possible.

### What this unlocks

- **[MFRL — 27, TD Methods Unified](../27-td-unified-view/index.qmd)** —
  Q-learning, Sarsa, Expected Sarsa as targets in one formula.
- **[MFRL — 28, From Table to Function](../28-from-table-to-function/index.qmd)** — when $q$ becomes a parameterised function.
- **[MFRL — 31, DQN](../31-deep-q-network/index.qmd)** — Q-learning with a
  deep network.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Q-learning is the foundation of every value-based deep-RL
agent: DQN, Double DQN, Rainbow, IQN, R2D2, Agent57. The off-policy
property is what enables *experience replay* — store transitions in a
buffer and learn from them later, regardless of the current policy.
See [Bellman equation](/glossary.qmd#bellman-equation) and
[expected value](/glossary.qmd#expected-value).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this track
expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Source:** Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement
  Learning* (Springer 2025), Chapter 7 §7.4. Cited freely; never transcribed.
- **Touchstones honoured:** Russian school problem-first sequencing,
  Better Explained "one symbol changes everything" framing, Bhāskara
  verse for the off-policy step.
- **Self-study guarantees:** pre-flight ✓, multiple representations
  (widget, recursion, English, grid) ✓, graduated hints ✓, worked
  solutions ✓, common-confusions ✓, self-assessment ✓.
:::
